mappings.rationaleAI: Mexico's coverage of the 1910-20 Revolution, post-revolutionary one-party PRI rule, democratic transition in 2000, and the border economy with the United States directly addresses Mexico since 1920 (A), including political and economic developments under the PRI, emergence as an oil producer, and fall of PRI with election of Vicente Fox (A.4).

The United States as a global superpower connects to Mexico's relations with the US including tensions over illegal immigration and NAFTA (A.4.b), US military intervention in Nicaragua (B.1.d), and US relations with Cuba including the trade embargo, Bay of Pigs, Cuban Missile Crisis, and emigration waves (B.2.c.iv).

Argentina's coverage of military dictatorships, the Peronist political era, the 1976-83 Proceso, and post-democracy economic volatility directly addresses Argentina since 1930 (F.1), the conservative restoration (F.1.a), the Peron era and his rise and fall (F.1.b), and Argentina since 1955 including military dictatorships, the Falkland Islands invasion, and return to civilian rule (F.1.c).

Brazil's coverage of Vargas-era populism, military dictatorship (1964-85), and post-1988 democratic consolidation directly addresses Brazil since 1930 as the Second Republic (G), the Vargas dictatorship including social and economic legislation and Allied participation in WWII (G.1), and political, social, and economic developments since 1945 (G.2).

The Cold War's proxy wars and ideological competition connect to Cuba's alignment with the Soviet bloc and the Cuban Missile Crisis (B.2.c), and US sponsorship of the Contra insurgency in Nicaragua (B.1.d).

The Great Depression's global economic impact connects to its effect on Bolivia's mining industry and the Chaco War (D.3.a), Chile's economic crises during the 1930s (E.1), and Paraguay's Great Depression impact and the Chaco War (F.3.a).

Colombia's coverage of independence, 20th-21st century armed conflict, and drug trafficking directly addresses Colombia since 1930 (C.2), Liberal Party rule and reforms (C.2.a), the Conservative rule and La Violencia (C.2.b), and increasing political violence, guerrillas, and drug trafficking (C.2.c).
Central America as a subregion of seven states with coverage of Cold War conflicts, US interventionism, and migration directly addresses the Central American republics since c. 1920 (B.1).

WWII's relevance connects to Mexico's economic and military cooperation with the United States during wartime (A.3), Brazil's Allied participation in WWII under Vargas (G.1), and Argentina's neutrality in World War II during the conservative restoration (F.1.a).

Decolonisation's coverage of post-WWII independence movements in the Caribbean connects to the new nations of the Caribbean region achieving independence and efforts toward development and regional cooperation (B.2.d).

North American history spanning post-war developments and Mexico's 20th-century trajectory connects broadly to Mexico since 1920 (A), providing regional context for Mexican political and economic evolution.

Imperialism as the extension of power over foreign peoples connects to US military intervention in Nicaragua (B.1.d) and US involvement in Cuba including nationalization, trade embargo, Bay of Pigs, and missile crisis (B.2.c.iv) as manifestations of American interventionism in Latin America.
The United Kingdom connects to British insular possessions in the Caribbean region (B.2.d) and Argentina's invasion of the Falkland Islands and defeat by Britain in 1982 (F.1.c).

The British Empire's Caribbean colonies connect to the new nations of the Caribbean region achieving independence from British colonial rule (B.2.d) and British insular possessions including Bermuda and other territories (B.2.e).

The Caribbean as a maritime region shaped by colonisation, the Atlantic slave trade, plantation economies, and decolonisation directly addresses the island states of the Caribbean since the end of US occupation (B.2) and the new nations of the Caribbean region including efforts toward development and regional cooperation (B.2.d).

Dictatorship as non-democratic government with military, one-party, and personalist subtypes connects to successive dictatorships across the Central American republics (B.1), the Peron era in Argentina including his rise and fall from power (F.1.b), and the Vargas dictatorship in Brazil (G.1).

South American history covering independence movements, Cold War-era military dictatorships, and the political economy of resource-dependent nations provides broad context for Venezuela and Colombia (C), Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia (D), Chile (E), Argentina, Uruguay, and Paraguay (F), and Brazil (G).
